EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex, broken line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of an approaching cold front producing very heavy rainfall and some embedded severe storms. Trees were blown trees onto power lines, which caused a number of power outages.  Henry County and the city of Martinsville suffered the most tree damage and power outages. Rainfall was extremely intense within the very moist environment with precipitable water over 2 inches and several boundary collisions enhancing the rainfall. The highest amounts occurred in the City of Lynchburg and adjacent parts of Campbell County where radar estimated 4 to 6 inches of rain in several hours which was confirmed by several rain gages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of roads washed out or overtopped in the Forest area  including Spring Lake Road, Bethel Church Road, Terry Place and Goose Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a negatively-tilted upper level trough approached southwest Virginia.  Plenty of wind shear with modest instability made conditions ripe for large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes.  The thunderstorms ahead of the cold front became strong enough to produce six tornadoes across portions of southwest Virginia.  While the first tornado originally formed in Caswell County of North Carolina and crossed into Virginia, the other five all touched down in Virginia.  These tornadoes caused significant damage within the cities of Danville and Lynchburg and within the town of Elon in Amherst County.  It is the first time ever since storm data records began in 1950 that a tornado struck inside the city limits of Lynchburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the third and final segment of a tornado that touched down just south of Waterlink Road near Timberlake in Campbell County at 6:00 PM EST. The tornado crossed through the independent city of Lynchburg and eventually tracked over the James River into Amherst County at 6:08 PM EST.  This was only the third EF3 tornado to occur in the NWS Blacksburg forecast since 1950, and was the longest track tornado within the state of Virginia for the NWS Blacksburg forecast area. The tornado reached its peak intensity with estimated maximum winds of 150 MPH as it crossed Route 130 and entered the town of Elon.  A total of 166 homes suffered damage, and 22 of those homes were considered a total loss.  A motor home was carried about thirty feet and tipped over.  The hardest hit locations were along Nottaway Drive and Deerfield Drive.  Seven people were injured and taken to the hospital from Elon.  As the tornado continued north-northeastward out of Elon, it snapped or uprooted hundreds of trees and caused more minor roof damage to homes.  At 6:25 PM EST, the tornado finally lifted as it began to head up Shady Mountain Ridge, which is about five and a half miles west-northwest of Amherst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation north of I-66 and west of I-81, and mainly rain in central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread ice accretion of around a quarter of an inch occurred across the county. In addition, between 1 and 2 inches of snow and sleet accumulated.

A strong cold front propelled by a deep upper trough that took on a negative tilt, pushed through the region on April 15 producing severe weather and heavy rainfall. The speed of convective elements prevented any flash flooding but longer duration flooding occurred on several rivers and streams, mainly in the minor category. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches with locally higher amounts up to nearly 4.5 inches in 24-hours ending at 7 AM on the 16th. The highest amounts were roughly a 5-year recurrence interval (0.2 Annual exceedance probability).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Staffordsville (STFV2) measured 2.81 inches for the 24-hour period ending 7AM on the 16th. This was the highest 1-day April rainfall total in the period of record at this location, with data starting in 1951.

A roof was blown off a garage, and a manufactured home was moved off its foundation.  In addition, a metal roof from a large shed was also peeled off by the tornado.  The estimated maximum winds reached 90 MPH.  By 5:59 PM EST, the tornado lifted just south of the intersection of New Chapel Road and Bethany Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed in the DC/Baltimore corridor during the afternoon of July 17th ahead of an approaching cold front. Rainfall of 1 to 3 inches caused flooding during rush hour, mainly due to standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 25 vehicles were damaged after heavy rains overwhelmed storm drains, forcing a buildup of water in a low area along George Washington Memorial Parkway near Reagan National Airport. 40 people were rescued from their vehicles during the evening rush hour. The road was impassible for a period until drainage could catch up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure moved up the Atlantic seaboard. Copious moisture spread northward across the southern and western suburbs of Washington DC in the state of Virginia. Rainfall totals of 3 to 6 inches were widespread, causing fairly widespread flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple stream gages on Cameron Run exceeded their flood stages during the time indicated. The gage at the Metro Yellow Line bridge near Huntington exceeded flood stage during the entire time indicated, while gages upstream exceeded flood stage for a shorter period of time. The main impact was to trails along the stream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the area on the 14th. The low tracked along a boundary that separated warm and humid air to the south and west from cooler marine air to the north and east. The warm and humid air led to an unstable atmosphere, and there was enough instability for storms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were down along Gordon Road near Smith Station Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prosperity Road was closed due to high water near the intersection with Morningside Road. Prosperity Avenue was closed due to flooding at Morningside Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 21st, the influence of an area of high pressure weakened, while the influence of an approaching frontal boundary strengthened. The result was the development of isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms. One of these storms increased to severe levels and generated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew two trees down and many tree limbs down within about a one and one-half mile wide area about two miles north of the Virginia, North Carolina state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area was within a region of moist and unstable air. Numerous heavy rain producing showers and storms developed across the area. One of these storms became strong and produced penny-size hail in Appomattox County.

Additional heavy rain during the evening of the 22nd caused more flooding which continued into the 23rd, and the combination of all the rain events created stream flooding that continued through the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River Road was closed due to flooding between Octonia Road and Dead End. Mutton Hollow road was closed due to flooding between Dyke Road and Turkey Ridge Road. Octonia Road was closed due to flooding between Ford Avenue and River road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on saturated soils during the evening of June 21st caused flooding. The heavy rains intensified overnight, causing both flooding and flash flooding early on the 22nd. Widespread flooding persisted throughout Northern Virginia throughout the day n the 22nd. Additional heavy rain during the evening of the 22nd caused more flooding which continued into the 23rd, and the combination of all the rain events created stream flooding that continued through the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Raccoon Ford Road flooded and closed on the south side of the Rapidan River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing flooding from June 2nd continued into the early morning hours of June 3rd. Then additional rainfall caused fairly widespread flooding across Northern Virginia later on June 3rd. Separately, the combined effects of several days of rain created river flooding on streams which continued in some locations through June 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred along Passage Creek between Buckton and Fort Valley, including multiple spots on Fort Valley Road. The stream gage on Passage Creek exceeded the 9 foot flood stage during the indicated times, with a peak observed of 12.00 feet at 6:45pm EST on June 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing flooding from June 2nd continued into the early morning hours of June 3rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along Opequon Creek in Clarke County both upstream and downstream of the stream gage near Berryville. The gage exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the indicated period, with a peak of 13.12 feet at 12:45pm EST on June 3rd. Opequon Road and Old Charles Town Road both flooded and other flooding, especially agricultural flooding, was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the Rapidan River near Culpeper exceeded the 13 foot flood stage during the indicated period. The peak level of 14.46 feet occurred at 2:00am EST on June 4th. River Road was flooded around the time of peak, along with farmland on both the Orange and Culpeper County sides of the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the area on the 10th. Warm and humid air ahead of the boundary led to an unstable atmosphere. The instability combined with forcing from the cold front to trigger a few severe thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing flooding from June 2nd continued into the early morning hours of June 3rd. Then additional rainfall caused fairly widespread flooding across Northern Virginia later on June 3rd. Separately, the combined effects of several days of rain created river flooding on streams which continued in some locations through June 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the North Fork Shenandoah River near Strasburg exceeded the 17 foot flood stage during the period indicated. The peak level was estimated by USGS to be 18.13 feet at 3:30am EST on June 4th. Deer Rapids Road floods along with significant agricultural flooding and flooding of the riverside park in Strasburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing flooding from June 2nd continued into the early morning hours of June 3rd. Then additional rainfall caused fairly widespread flooding across Northern Virginia later on June 3rd. Separately, the combined effects of several days of rain created river flooding on streams which continued in some locations through June 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Cedar Creek near Winchester exceeded the 16 foot flood stage during the indicated period. The peak level of 16.32 feet occurred at 3:00pm EST on June 3rd. Fromans Road was flooded and water neared an outbuilding nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the area on the 14th. The low tracked along a boundary that separated warm and humid air to the south and west from cooler marine air to the north and east. The warm and humid air led to an unstable atmosphere, and there was enough instability for storms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down near the intersection of Tasker Road and Lakeside Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell during the evening and early overnight of May 16th in extreme northern Virginia, near Winchester. This heavy rain of 1-3 inches caused flooding in Clarke, Frederick, and Loudoun counties early on the 17th. More heavy rain developed in Central Virginia during the afternoon of the 17th, causing new flooding as a result of 1-4 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water over Garber Road alongside Mill Creek, rendering the roadway impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell during the evening and early overnight of May 16th in extreme northern Virginia, near Winchester. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on Opequon Creek near Berryville exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the indicated times. The peak observed was 11.54 feet at 8:30am EST on May 17th. At the observed levels, Old Charles Town Road is flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-3 inches fell during the evening and overnight of May 18th into May 19th along and east of the Blue Ridge Mountains. This prompted flooding in the region, mainly of streams. Some of the stream flooding lasted into the 20th, especially on larger rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Cedar Run near Aden exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the times indicated. The peak level of 10.06 feet occurred at 2:25pm EST on May 19th. Emergency management confirmed Aden Road was flooded with around 6 inches of water near the gage location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered heavy afternoon thunderstorms produced 1-2 inches of rain, causing flooding in a few different locations in northern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carrico Mills Road flooded and closed near Mountain Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on saturated soils during the evening of June 21st caused flooding. The heavy rains intensified overnight, causing both flooding and flash flooding early on the 22nd. Widespread flooding persisted throughout Northern Virginia throughout the day n the 22nd. Additional heavy rain during the evening of the 22nd caused more flooding which continued into the 23rd, and the combination of all the rain events created stream flooding that continued through the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Proffit Road was washed out due to flooding of the Rivanna River in the 1700 block.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Duet Road was closed due to flooding between Whippoorwill Road and Dead End.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters were covering the road near the entrance to the Farmington Heights neighborhood. This forced local residents to be told to shelter in place since they could not leave the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on saturated soils during the evening of June 21st caused flooding. The heavy rains intensified overnight, causing both flooding and flash flooding early on the 22nd. Widespread flooding persisted throughout Northern Virginia throughout the day n the 22nd. Additional heavy rain during the evening of the 22nd caused more flooding which continued into the 23rd, and the combination of all the rain events created stream flooding that continued through the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beahm Town Road flooded and closed at the Robinson River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly sagged southward through Maryland during the evening hours of May 15th. Stream flooding continued into the early morning hours of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-3 inches fell during the evening and overnight of May 18th into May 19th along and east of the Blue Ridge Mountains. This prompted flooding in the region, mainly of streams. Some of the stream flooding lasted into the 20th, especially on larger rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Bull Run near Manassas Park exceeded the 12 foot flood stage during the indicated times. The peak level of 12.44 feet was observed at 2:18pm EST on May 19th. Emergency management confirmed flooding of backyards along Lake Drive, and flooding of nearby trails.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bear Wallow Road was closed due to flooding of Cattail Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surging warm front spread moderate to heavy rain across the region. Dry conditions prior to the event served to limit the amount of flooding, but 1.5-2 inches of rain was enough to cause some street flooding in more urbanized areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain caused rapidly rising standing water in the unit block of West Glebe Road. The water reportedly reached three feet or more in depth, forcing closure of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope winds on the east side of the Blue Ridge just north of an approaching warm front aided in producing torrential rainfall and catastrophic flooding in parts of Central Virginia. Rainfall amounts of 4-10 inches occurred north and west of Charlottesville. Later in the evening, heavy rain developed in northern Virginia, with 1-3 inches in the southern and western suburbs of Washington DC, as well as again near Harrisonburg. This produced another round of flooding and some flash flooding that actually continued into June 1st. June Storm Data will contain those details when available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A driver had to be rescued when the vehicle became stranded in two to three feet of standing water on US Highway 29 near Plank Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation north of I-66 and west of I-81, and mainly rain in central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of around a tenth of an inch of ice were observed, along with 1 to 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a coastal low, bands of moisture continued to pull northward across the same areas that had already experienced heavy rain. Heavy rainfall fell in some areas, with widespread flooding throughout the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bellview Road flooded and closed near Rocky Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope winds on the east side of the Blue Ridge just north of an approaching warm front aided in producing torrential rainfall and catastrophic flooding in parts of Central Virginia. Rainfall amounts of 4-10 inches occurred north and west of Charlottesville. Later in the evening, heavy rain developed in northern Virginia, with 1-3 inches in the southern and western suburbs of Washington DC, as well as again near Harrisonburg. This produced another round of flooding and some flash flooding that actually continued into June 1st. June Storm Data will contain those details when available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on 10th Street near South Kirby Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 690 at Cannonball Gate Road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge off the east coast and an upper low over the central U.S. combined to bring enhanced moisture into the region. Weak low pressure along a frontal boundary draped near the Virginia-North Carolina border provided additional convergence and lift to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms mainly over the piedmont and Blue Ridge foothills. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches across parts of Patrick, Franklin and Henry counties most of which fell in about a 3-hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blackberry Creek flooded portions of Route 698 west of Bassett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved up the eastern seaboard of the United States from November 26th through the 27th, with cold temperatures and strong northwest winds funneling behind the system from the night of the 27th into the morning of the 28th. This was able to produce wind chill values as low as -10 degrees over the higher terrain near the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and strong winds produced wind chills as low as -10 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure remained to our west and a southeast flow continued to pump in copious amounts of moisture. A few thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary to the south, and there was enough low-level rotation for an isolated thunderstorm to produce a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Baltimore MD/Washington DC confirmed an EF-0 tornado at Thomas Jefferson High School in Lincolnia Virginia.||The tornado touched down briefly just south of the softball field, damaging fences, two sheds, light poles and several trees. A shipping container was lofted over 100 yards as the tornado crossed the softball field moving northeast over an adjacent|athletic field. The tornado then lifted over the northeastern part of the school property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the area on the 27th. Ahead of the boundary, a southerly flow led to plenty of moisture an unstable atmosphere. A potent upper-level low caused stronger winds aloft as well. Some thunderstorms associated with the cold front became severe due to the instability and stronger winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were down with the motorist trapped between the intersection of Old Bust Head road and Fosters Fork Road. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on May 21st in western Rockingham County caused significant flooding. Those floodwaters moved downstream on the 22nd, causing stream flooding. Meanwhile, afternoon showers and thunderstorms produced around an inch of rain, producing some urban flooding in the DC metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the North Fork Shenandoah River at Mount Jackson exceeded the 13 foot flood stage during the times indicated. The peak level of 14.67 feet was the highest since 2011, and was observed at 9:00am EST on May 22nd. At these levels, water approaches roads near the river in Mount Jackson and agricultural flooding is occurring.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Autumn Ridge Circle flooded near Twisted Oak Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seneca Road was closed due to flooding near Kentland Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough with a slow-moving frontal boundary brought an extended period of mainly light to moderate rainfall that began on the afternoon of the 10th and persisted well into the evening of the 11th. Several rivers reached flood stage and a number of roads were reported closed. Rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 3 inches in a 24-hour period ending at 7 AM EST on the 11th and for many locations it was one of the wettest February days on record. 48-hour storm total rainfall (ending 7 AM EST) on the 12th) reached 4 to 5 inches in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dan River at South Boston crested at 19.96 feet just over Minor Flood Stage (19 ft.) during the evening of the 12th. The Roanoke (Staunton) River also exceeded minor flood stage in far eastern Halifax County along the Charlotte County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope winds on the east side of the Blue Ridge just north of an approaching warm front aided in producing torrential rainfall and catastrophic flooding in parts of Central Virginia. Rainfall amounts of 4-10 inches occurred north and west of Charlottesville. Later in the evening, heavy rain developed in northern Virginia, with 1-3 inches in the southern and western suburbs of Washington DC, as well as again near Harrisonburg. This produced another round of flooding and some flash flooding that actually continued into June 1st. June Storm Data will contain those details when available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rosebrook Road was reported flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation north of I-66 and west of I-81, and mainly rain in central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred, along with a light accumulation of ice to a few hundredths of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the area on the 10th. Warm and humid air ahead of the boundary led to an unstable atmosphere. The instability combined with forcing from the cold front to trigger a few severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on Pheasant Run causing a gas line to rupture and a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the area on the 14th. The low tracked along a boundary that separated warm and humid air to the south and west from cooler marine air to the north and east. The warm and humid air led to an unstable atmosphere, and there was enough instability for storms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along Old Battlefield Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through on the 17th, and southwest winds ahead of the boundary led to an unstable atmosphere. The instability combined with the lift from the cold front to produced a severe thunderstorm.

One of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds near Bluefield, VA.  Another thunderstorm, while not severe, produced lightning that struck a shed in Troutdale, VA, setting it on fire causing total loss of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell during the evening and early overnight of May 16th in extreme northern Virginia, near Winchester. This heavy rain of 1-3 inches caused flooding in Clarke, Frederick, and Loudoun counties early on the 17th. More heavy rain developed in Central Virginia during the afternoon of the 17th, causing new flooding as a result of 1-4 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on South Fork Catoctin Creek near Waterford exceeded the 8 foot flood stage during the indicated times. The peak level of 8.49 feet occurred at 11:00am EST on May 17th. At these levels, agricultural overbank flooding is occurring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent longwave trough approached the area from the west on the 20th. Warm advection and strong moisture transport ahead of the mid-upper level wave led to a prolonged period of rainfall through the day as surface low pressure tracked from the FL Panhandle to WV by evening. Rainfall totals on the order of 1-2 inches led to instances of flooding across portions of Augusta, Nelson and Albemarle Counties in VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Middle River at Grottoes exceeded the 12 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Fields along the river were flooded. The peak level of 12.24 feet occurred at 2:00 PM on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on Tye Brook Highway and Route 56.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and storms developed with a hot and humid summer air mass across the region. One of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds near Bluefield, VA.  Another thunderstorm, while not severe, produced lightning that struck a shed in Troutdale, VA, setting it on fire causing total loss of the structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning was the probable cause for a fire that destroyed a shed within the 100 block of Ottaway Road in Troutdale.  The shed housed a chicken coop, and all birds escaped unharmed. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation north of I-66 and west of I-81, and mainly rain in central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of between a trace and a tenth of an inch were observed.

Rainfall totals on the order of 1-2 inches led to instances of flooding across portions of Augusta, Nelson and Albemarle Counties in VA.

Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches across parts of Patrick, Franklin and Henry counties most of which fell in about a 3-hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the North Fork Shenandoah River at Cootes Store exceeded the 15 foot flood stage during the times indicated. The peak level of 17.00 feet was observed at 1:15am EST on May 22nd, and was the highest level observed since 1996. This was also the first flood of any kind at this location since 2011. At these levels, agricultural flooding is occurring, along with flooding of a campground. Low spots on Brocks Gap road also begin to flood.

Rainfall amounts of 4-10 inches occurred north and west of Charlottesville. Later in the evening, heavy rain developed in northern Virginia, with 1-3 inches in the southern and western suburbs of Washington DC, as well as again near Harrisonburg. This produced another round of flooding and some flash flooding that actually continued into June 1st. June Storm Data will contain those details when available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two women attempted to cross the swollen Rose River at Graves Mountain Lodge in an ATV. When one of the women got out to check on the water, she was knocked off her feet and swept away. The other woman was uninjured and rescued.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A EF-0 tornado touched down 2 miles south of the city limits of|the City of Charlottesville in the Avon Park area of Albemarle|County. The touchdown point at 11:00 AM was near the intersection|of Avon Street Extension and Scottsville Road. The tornado |tracked northeast, roughly parallel to Scottsville Road. Many |trees were uprooted or had large portions snapped off. There was |some minor structural damage to Monticello High School involving |windows and doors. Also at the school, trees and parking lot |lights were downed. A chimney was toppled onto the roof of a home |on Galaxie Farm Lane. The tornado dissipated 5 minutes later |as it crossed near the intersection of Willow Lake Drive and |Scottsville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a negatively-tilted upper level trough approached southwest Virginia.  Plenty of wind shear with modest instability made conditions ripe for large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes.  The thunderstorms ahead of the cold front became strong enough to produce six tornadoes across portions of southwest Virginia.  While the first tornado originally formed in Caswell County of North Carolina and crossed into Virginia, the other five all touched down in Virginia.  These tornadoes caused significant damage within the cities of Danville and Lynchburg and within the town of Elon in Amherst County.  It is the first time ever since storm data records began in 1950 that a tornado struck inside the city limits of Lynchburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the second segment of a tornado that touched down just south of Waterlink Road near Timberlake in Campbell County at 6:00 PM EST.  The tornado crossed Timberlake Road and entered the independent city of Lynchburg at 6:03 PM EST.  The tornado uprooted trees and caused notable damage to 332 private buildings, and 34 of those buildings were condemned due to the damage.  It also damaged numerous power lines and transformers.  Two people were injured.  At 6:07 PM EST, the tornado left the independent city of Lynchburg, crossed the James River, and continued northward into Amherst County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lee Highway was closed due to high water at the intersection with Sudley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope winds on the east side of the Blue Ridge just north of an approaching warm front aided in producing torrential rainfall and catastrophic flooding in parts of Central Virginia. Rainfall amounts of 4-10 inches occurred north and west of Charlottesville. Later in the evening, heavy rain developed in northern Virginia, with 1-3 inches in the southern and western suburbs of Washington DC, as well as again near Harrisonburg. This produced another round of flooding and some flash flooding that actually continued into June 1st. June Storm Data will contain those details when available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ford Avenue flooded and closed near the substation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the area during the early morning hours of the 28th. Warm and moist air associated with a tropical air mass overran the relatively cooler air in place, resulting in heavy rainfall. Also, the steering flow aloft was weak so showers and thunderstorms were slow to move. This enhanced the flooding threat. Some of the flooding persisted on larger streams into the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 22000 Block of Route 11 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked eastward along the Gulf Coast and spread moisture northward toward the Mid Atlantic.  Meanwhile, high pressure to the north allowed temperatures to fall into the 20s, which caused the moisture to mostly fall as heavy snow.  Some sleet and freezing drizzle also fell during this storm.  The snow caused hundreds of traffic accidents and left thousands of people without power.  Average snowfall accumulations ranged from ten to twenty inches over southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 15.0 inches was reported at Covington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northeast just off the southeast and Mid Atlantic coasts produced snowfall totals between three inches and fourteen inches across central, south central, and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged between six inches and twelve inches across the county. Hopewell (1 NE) reported 12.0 inches of snow. Prince George Golf Course (1 N) reported 10.5 inches of snow. Petersburg (1 S) reported 9.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked eastward along the Gulf Coast and spread moisture northward toward the Mid Atlantic.  Meanwhile, high pressure to the north allowed temperatures to fall into the 20s, which caused the moisture to mostly fall as heavy snow.  Some sleet and freezing drizzle also fell during this storm.  The snow caused hundreds of traffic accidents and left thousands of people without power.  Average snowfall accumulations ranged from ten to twenty inches over southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 11.0 inches about two miles east of Forest to 15.0 inches at Rustburg.  Lynchburg received 11.7 inches of snow, which is the third highest storm total in the month of December since records began in 1893. It is also the earliest in the winter season that an amount this high has ever been recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the area during the early morning hours of the 28th. Warm and moist air associated with a tropical air mass overran the relatively cooler air in place, resulting in heavy rainfall. Also, the steering flow aloft was weak so showers and thunderstorms were slow to move. This enhanced the flooding threat. Some of the flooding persisted on larger streams into the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bastable Mill Road was closed near Brookfield Drive due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked eastward along the Gulf Coast and spread moisture northward toward the Mid Atlantic.  Meanwhile, high pressure to the north allowed temperatures to fall into the 20s, which caused the moisture to mostly fall as heavy snow.  Some sleet and freezing drizzle also fell during this storm.  The snow caused hundreds of traffic accidents and left thousands of people without power.  Average snowfall accumulations ranged from ten to twenty inches over southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 12.0 inches at Richlands to 15.0 inches at Burkes Garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third late December significant rainfall event developed after Christmas as the active southern jet stream helped to spin up another complex area of low pressure which slowly moved through the southeast and Mid-Atlantic December 27-29. Rainfall over the 2-day period ending at 12z (7 AM EST) on the 29th was approximately 1 to 3 inches across the region with isolated higher amounts up to 3.5 inches.  Once again the Dan and lower Roanoke (Staunton) rivers rose back above flood stage, although levels were slightly lower than the two previous events in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Route 60 was closed due to flooding at a bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rain event occurred December 15-16 as a closed upper low and associated surface system tracked from the lower Mississippi Valley across the mid-Atlantic region. This brought two-day rain amounts  ranging  from  0.75  to  1.50  inches,  with  isolated  amounts over  2  inches.  Some  river  flooding  ensued due to the rain and snowmelt runoff, primarily along the Dan and lower Roanoke (Staunton) rivers. Minor to low-end moderate  flooding occurred from  Danville  downstream  to  South  Boston  and  from  Brookneal  to  Randolph  on  the  Roanoke  (Staunton) River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Glasgow 1 SE (GLAV2) reported 1.98 inches for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM local time which was the highest on record for December 15th. The old record was 0.80 inches in 1981. Climate records at this station began in 1967.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third late December significant rainfall event developed after Christmas as the active southern jet stream helped to spin up another complex area of low pressure which slowly moved through the southeast and Mid-Atlantic December 27-29. Rainfall over the 2-day period ending at 12z (7 AM EST) on the 29th was approximately 1 to 3 inches across the region with isolated higher amounts up to 3.5 inches.  Once again the Dan and lower Roanoke (Staunton) rivers rose back above flood stage, although levels were slightly lower than the two previous events in December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the area during the early morning hours of the 28th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another southern stream dominated system emerged from the southwestern U.S. with a complex surface low tracking from the Gulf Coast into the southern Mid-Atlantic December 20-22. This was again an all liquid event, with 2-day rainfall totals (ending 700 AM local time on 12/22) ranging from 1 to 4 inches with isolated higher amounts mainly along and near the Blue Ridge. The top five rainfall reports in southwest Virginia in this period included Jones Creek IFLOWS (JOEV2) 4.34 inches, Fancy Gap IFLOWS (FYGV2) 4.10 inches, Glasgow 1 SE COOP (GLAV2) 4.06 inches, Trappers Lodge IFLOWS (TRAV2) 3.71 inches and Pipers Gaps IFLOWS (PPGV2) 3.71 inches. Flooding very similar in magnitude and location to the mid-December event occurred with most of the Dan and lower Roanoke (Staunton) again reaching minor to low-end moderate flood levels. Minor flooding also occurred along portions of the New River with the gage at Alisonia (ALSV2) reaching minor flood stage of 8 feet in the evening of the 21st flooding a few roads near the river. In addition, a number of areal Flood Advisories and Warnings were issued for numerous counties after reports of flooded roads were received mainly on the evening of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Glasgow 1 SE (GLAV2) reported 3.57 inches for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM local time which was the highest on record for December  21st. The old record was 2.72 inches in 1973. It was also the 3rd highest daily precipitation recorded in the month of December. The record is 3.90 inches set December 1, 2010. Climate records at this station began in 1967.

The top five rainfall reports in southwest Virginia in this period included Jones Creek IFLOWS (JOEV2) 4.34 inches, Fancy Gap IFLOWS (FYGV2) 4.10 inches, Glasgow 1 SE COOP (GLAV2) 4.06 inches, Trappers Lodge IFLOWS (TRAV2) 3.71 inches and Pipers Gaps IFLOWS (PPGV2) 3.71 inches. Flooding very similar in magnitude and location to the mid-December event occurred with most of the Dan and lower Roanoke (Staunton) again reaching minor to low-end moderate flood levels. Minor flooding also occurred along portions of the New River with the gage at Alisonia (ALSV2) reaching minor flood stage of 8 feet in the evening of the 21st flooding a few roads near the river. In addition, a number of areal Flood Advisories and Warnings were issued for numerous counties after reports of flooded roads were received mainly on the evening of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lone Jacks Road near Natural Bridge was closed due to flooding from the Maury River with water across the road. Some streets in Buena Vista were also flooded. Portions of Tinkerville Road were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the area during the early morning hours of the 28th. Warm and moist air associated with a tropical air mass overran the relatively cooler air in place, resulting in heavy rainfall. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance tracked eastward from the Ohio Valley in the morning of December 5th. This moved across the region during the morning hours, aided by the left front quadrant of an upper jetmax. The disturbance then exited during the afternoon hours. Areas of light snow moved across portions of the region, although meaningful accumulations were confined to the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation north of I-66 and west of I-81, and mainly rain in central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches occurred, with a local maximum of 3.7 measured in Woolsey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed along the Gulf Coast on the night of November 23rd, and tracked northeastward to the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 24th, strengthening to around 990mb on the night of the 24th as it continued moving northeastward through the Delmarva to off the coast of New Jersey by the morning of the 25th. The system then began weakening on the 25th as it moved out into the western Atlantic Ocean. Widespread rain was brought to the region, along with a period of freezing rain and ice accumulations along and west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of up to a tenth of an inch were observed across the higher elevations of the Central Virginia Blue Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked northeastward through the Appalachians as a potent jetstreak in the upper levels shifted northward. Unseasonably warm and moist air was transported northward and this, in combination with colder air moving in at the mid-levels, caused the |atmosphere to destabilize, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. A few of these storms became locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on a car on US-15 James Monroe Highway near VA-651 Hogback Mountain Road. A second tree was also blown down on US-15 near VA-733 Lime Kiln Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low and associated area of surface low pressure approached the region from southwest on the 15th. Strong forcing for ascent and ample moisture transport in advance of the upper level low led to a prolonged period of rain across the region. Eventually low pressure transferred to the coast before precipitation wound down on the 16th. Widespread rainfall totals of 2-4 inches were observed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: VA-642 (Duet Road) closed due to high water near VA-603 (Whippoorwill Road). VA-600 Bohannon Road closed due to high water between Old Blue Ridge Turnpike and Nethers Road. VA-602 (Hughes River Road) closed due to high water near VA-231 (S F T Valley Road). VA-644 (Emmett Road) closed due to high water near VA-646 (Champe Plain Road).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent longwave trough approached the area from the west on the 20th. Warm advection and strong moisture transport ahead of the mid-upper level wave led to a prolonged period of rainfall through the day as surface low pressure tracked from the FL Panhandle to WV by evening. Rainfall totals on the order of 1-2 inches led to instances of flooding across portions of Augusta, Nelson and Albemarle Counties in VA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of water flowing across US-250 (Jefferson Highway) between Desper Hollow Road and VA-637 (Jericho Road).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough approached the area on the 24th as surface low pressure tracked up toward the Great Lakes and secondary low pressure formed along the Carolina Coast. Lift ahead of the negatively tilted trough/within the left exit region of an upper-level jet streak led to a period of moderate to heavy rain through much of the day on the 24th. Rainfall totals were generally between 1-2 inches across the area, resulting in many instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both eastbound lanes of Lee Highway were closed at Stringfellow Road due to floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low and associated area of surface low pressure approached the region from southwest on the 15th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Hurricane Michael made landfall near Mexico Beach, Florida with maximum sustained winds of 155 mph and a minimum central pressure of 919 mb on Wednesday October 10th, it weakened to a tropical storm as it tracked northeastward through Georgia and South Carolina. The tropical storm then tracked across North Carolina and southeast Virginia Thursday and Thursday night, October 11th, emerging back into the Atlantic Ocean early Friday morning, October 12th. Ahead of the tropical storm, the strong low level wind field combined with some instability ahead of the storm to generate bands of showers and thunderstorms, one of which became severe. As the system passed south of the region, heavy rain then caused instances of flooding, and strong winds brought down trees. Across Virginia, maximum sustained winds reached 35 mph, peak wind gusts reached 46 mph, and some rainfall totals were observed in excess of 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down in the 11400 block of Catharpin Road between Catharpin Landing Road and Piney Branch Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall total of 9.12 inches was reported at Bowling Green (2 NE). Rainfall total of 5.16 inches was reported at Port Royal (2 S). Rainfall total of 4.61 inches was reported at Fort A.P. Hill (1 SSW).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Michael made landfall along the Florida panhandle as major hurricane (Category 4 on the Saffir-Simpson scale) on Wednesday afternoon, October 10th, 2018, then tracked northeastward with the northern portion of the storm circulation tracking across portions of Southside, Virginia Thursday afternoon, the 11th. A deep flow of tropical moisture into the Appalachians and central mid Atlantic region ahead of the storm began to generate excessive rainfall across portions of the region as early as Wednesday evening, mainly along the Interstate 77 corridor, causing some degree of flash flooding and otherwise saturating the ground even before the arrival of the rainfall directly associated with Michael.||As the storm circulation approached on Thursday, a cold front moving in from the west and interacted with the storm and enhanced rainfall especially east of Interstate 81. Widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported, along with local amounts over 10 inches, mainly from the mountains of North Carolina up through Southside Virginia. This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville.  Unfortunately, there were five fatalities as vehicles were swept away by flash flood waters in Pittsylvania County, Danville, and Charlotte County, Virginia.||The massive volume of water also then generated rapid rises on rivers including the New, Roanoke, and Dan. Major flooding was reported at several river gage locations along with a couple of record crests. There were a few landslides reported as well, blocking roads and in one case destroying a shed.||In addition, as the northern portion of the storm circulation moved across the Piedmont, bands of heavier rains/convection developed and mixed strong winds down to the surface.  Many trees were blown down in the water logged soil especially across Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties of Virginia where rainfall was heaviest.  Despite some initial reports of tornado sightings, damage surveys revealed no conclusive evidence of tornadic circulations and judged damage was caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roanoke River at Glenvar (GNVV2) crested at 17.10 feet (18600 cfs) on the evening of the 11th. Major flood stage is 16 feet. Several roads were closed near the river but it is unclear if homes or structures were affected. It was the 4th highest reading at this location since USGS records began in 1992 and the highest since September 28, 2004 (12.45 feet) with the remains of Hurricane Jeanne. In terms of flood frequency it near 10-year event (0.10 annual chance flood) per USGS studies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, supported aloft by a fasting-moving and deep upper level disturbance passing across the central Appalachians, produced a brief but potent round of high winds across the ridges and foothills of the Blue Ridge of southern Virginia.  Lowering inversion levels associated with clearing skies and subsidence in the approaching high pressure exposed the affected area to 50 knot winds aloft working their way to the surface.  Strongest winds occurred late in the evening of October 20th and during the early morning hours of the 21st.  By sunrise on the 21st, wind speeds were already quickly diminishing as the upper level support moved away to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging wind gusts, estimated to be 40 mph to locally 60 mph, blew down at least twenty trees across the county.  These strong winds also caused scattered power outages across the county.

This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville.  Unfortunately, there were five fatalities as vehicles were swept away by flash flood waters in Pittsylvania County, Danville, and Charlotte County, Virginia.||The massive volume of water also then generated rapid rises on rivers including the New, Roanoke, and Dan. Major flooding was reported at several river gage locations along with a couple of record crests. There were a few landslides reported as well, blocking roads and in one case destroying a shed.||In addition, as the northern portion of the storm circulation moved across the Piedmont, bands of heavier rains/convection developed and mixed strong winds down to the surface.  Many trees were blown down in the water logged soil especially across Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties of Virginia where rainfall was heaviest.  Despite some initial reports of tornado sightings, damage surveys revealed no conclusive evidence of tornadic circulations and judged damage was caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer ay South Boston (SBNV2) measured 5.20 inches for the 24-hour period ending 700 AM local time, October 12, 2018. This was the 5th highest 24-hour rainfall on record for this station and easily the highest in October. Complete records date back to 1980 at this station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Michael tracked from South Carolina northeast and off the Mid Atlantic Coast from Thursday morning, October 11 into early Friday morning, October 12. Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with the Tropical Cyclone produced seven tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down by the Nottoway Correctional Center as an EF0. It then crossed Lewiston Plank Road, paralleling Route 360/460 where trees were snapped. The tornado did minor damage to Mimmo's Restaurant before crossing Business Route 360/460 into Burkeville, where the roof was blown off of Paris Inn Restaurant and a concrete block outbuilding collapsed. The tornado did its worst damage to a local auto repair shop on Cauthorne Street, where the roof was blown off and a portion of the back and side concrete block walls collapsed. There was extensive tree damage, as well as some damage to mobile homes at the same location on Cauthorne Street. The tornado then snapped some trees near Luck Stone Quarry before lifting near the intersection of Route 360/460 and Dutchtown Road.

Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with the Tropical Cyclone produced seven tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on Jennings Lane and traveled northwest where numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. Significant structural damage occurred to a 2 story home at the end of Jennings Lane. Significant uplift of the roof was noted along with the house slightly shifting on its foundation. A garage collapsed and an outbuilding was destroyed. Winds were rated around 110 mph. The tornado then lifted near the intersection of Namozine and Military Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Michael made landfall along the Florida panhandle as major hurricane (Category 4 on the Saffir-Simpson scale) on Wednesday afternoon, October 10th, 2018, then tracked northeastward with the northern portion of the storm circulation tracking across portions of Southside, Virginia Thursday afternoon, the 11th. A deep flow of tropical moisture into the Appalachians and central mid Atlantic region ahead of the storm began to generate excessive rainfall across portions of the region as early as Wednesday evening, mainly along the Interstate 77 corridor, causing some degree of flash flooding and otherwise saturating the ground even before the arrival of the rainfall directly associated with Michael.||As the storm circulation approached on Thursday, a cold front moving in from the west and interacted with the storm and enhanced rainfall especially east of Interstate 81. Widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported, along with local amounts over 10 inches, mainly from the mountains of North Carolina up through Southside Virginia. This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville.  Unfortunately, there were five fatalities as vehicles were swept away by flash flood waters in Pittsylvania County, Danville, and Charlotte County, Virginia.||The massive volume of water also then generated rapid rises on rivers including the New, Roanoke, and Dan. Major flooding was reported at several river gage locations along with a couple of record crests. There were a few landslides reported as well, blocking roads and in one case destroying a shed.||In addition, as the northern portion of the storm circulation moved across the Piedmont, bands of heavier rains/convection developed and mixed strong winds down to the surface.  Many trees were blown down in the water logged soil especially across Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties of Virginia where rainfall was heaviest.  Despite some initial reports of tornado sightings, damage surveys revealed no conclusive evidence of tornadic circulations and judged damage was caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts, the highest observed being a 52 mph gust measured along Hazelwood Terrace near the community of Stewartsville, were common during the passage of Tropical Storm Michael.  These gusty winds, when combined with the effects of saturated ground due to heavy rain from the storm, resulted in numerous trees and power lines down across the county.  Gusts as high as 32 mph were measured in the community of Moneta.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be in the 25 to 35 mph range were common during the passage of Tropical Storm Michael, though one mesonet site reported a gust of 52 mph at around 3:30 pm EDT. Trees fell on top of at least two homes and several automobiles in the City of Danville, causing extensive structural damage.  At one point, at least 20,000 residents were without power due to the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Hurricane Michael made landfall near Mexico Beach, Florida with maximum sustained winds of 155 mph and a minimum central pressure of 919 mb on Wednesday October 10th, it weakened to a tropical storm as it tracked northeastward through Georgia and South Carolina. The tropical storm then tracked across North Carolina and southeast Virginia Thursday and Thursday night, October 11th, emerging back into the Atlantic Ocean early Friday morning, October 12th. Ahead of the tropical storm, the strong low level wind field combined with some instability ahead of the storm to generate bands of showers and thunderstorms, one of which became severe. As the system passed south of the region, heavy rain then caused instances of flooding, and strong winds brought down trees. Across Virginia, maximum sustained winds reached 35 mph, peak wind gusts reached 46 mph, and some rainfall totals were observed in excess of 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto Tye River Road near Falling Rock Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Michael made landfall along the Florida panhandle as major hurricane (Category 4 on the Saffir-Simpson scale) on Wednesday afternoon, October 10th, 2018, then tracked northeastward with the northern portion of the storm circulation tracking across portions of Southside, Virginia Thursday afternoon, the 11th. A deep flow of tropical moisture into the Appalachians and central mid Atlantic region ahead of the storm began to generate excessive rainfall across portions of the region as early as Wednesday evening, mainly along the Interstate 77 corridor, causing some degree of flash flooding and otherwise saturating the ground even before the arrival of the rainfall directly associated with Michael.||As the storm circulation approached on Thursday, a cold front moving in from the west and interacted with the storm and enhanced rainfall especially east of Interstate 81. Widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported, along with local amounts over 10 inches, mainly from the mountains of North Carolina up through Southside Virginia. This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville.  Unfortunately, there were five fatalities as vehicles were swept away by flash flood waters in Pittsylvania County, Danville, and Charlotte County, Virginia.||The massive volume of water also then generated rapid rises on rivers including the New, Roanoke, and Dan. Major flooding was reported at several river gage locations along with a couple of record crests. There were a few landslides reported as well, blocking roads and in one case destroying a shed.||In addition, as the northern portion of the storm circulation moved across the Piedmont, bands of heavier rains/convection developed and mixed strong winds down to the surface.  Many trees were blown down in the water logged soil especially across Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties of Virginia where rainfall was heaviest.  Despite some initial reports of tornado sightings, damage surveys revealed no conclusive evidence of tornadic circulations and judged damage was caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Keysville 2 S (KYSV2) measured 9.02 inches for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM local time, October 12, 2018. This was the highest 24-hour rainfall amount at this station with partial data back to 1979. The old record was 5.50 inches in July, 2005. In addition, the 9.02 inches is the 3rd highest 24-hour amount for any COOP station in the climate record for the NWS Blacksburg. According to the observer, most of this rain fell in about a 3.5 hour period on the afternoon of the 11th. For the 24-hour duration 9.02 inches falls into the 90 percent confidence interval (8.19-10.2 inches) for a 200-year (.002 annual chance event). For a duration of even 6 hours (being very conservative) the rainfall frequency was in excess of the 1,000 year event (.001 annual chance).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Michael tracked from South Carolina northeast and off the Mid Atlantic Coast from Thursday morning, October 11 into early Friday morning, October 12. Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with the Tropical Cyclone produced seven tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the northern side of the Colonial Heritage Club just south of Norge. The tornado then tracked northwest toward Toano and downed several trees. One tree went through a house on Arthur Hill Road. A roof was blown off a house near Candle Station before the tornado lifted just to the east of Toano.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on Pampa Road in northern Gloucester County before moving to the north-northwest and downing numerous trees. The tornado then lifted near the intersection of Route 17 and Glenns Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pittsylvania County was included in a Presidential Major Disaster declaration due mainly to the severe flooding associated with Tropical Storm Michael. Rainfall of 4 to 8 inches in several hours triggered widespread flash flooding across the county with numerous roads closed and extensive damage.  A man lost his life when his automobile was washed off Sandy Creek Road near the 600 block during intense flooding along Sandy Creek.  Media reports indicate the incident occurred around 330 PM local time, with one witness observing that water was flowing rapidly at least two feet above the road surface.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New River flooded with the gage at Ivanhoe (IVNV2) rising above the Moderate flood stage of 19 feet, and reaching a peak of 20.05 feet (53100 cfs) on the afternoon of the 11th. This was the highest stage in over 40 years, since November 7, 1977 when the river reached 28.88 feet. According to USGS studies flooding from Michael was between a 10- and 25-year flood event (0.10 to 0.04 annual chance flood). Several roads close to the river were flooded and closed including Ivanhoe Road in Ivanhoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Hurricane Michael made landfall near Mexico Beach, Florida with maximum sustained winds of 155 mph and a minimum central pressure of 919 mb on Wednesday October 10th, it weakened to a tropical storm as it tracked northeastward through Georgia and South Carolina. The tropical storm then tracked across North Carolina and southeast Virginia Thursday and Thursday night, October 11th, emerging back into the Atlantic Ocean early Friday morning, October 12th. Ahead of the tropical storm, the strong low level wind field combined with some instability ahead of the storm to generate bands of showers and thunderstorms, one of which became severe. As the system passed south of the region, heavy rain then caused instances of flooding, and strong winds brought down trees. Across Virginia, maximum sustained winds reached 35 mph, peak wind gusts reached 46 mph, and some rainfall totals were observed in excess of 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of flooded roads across the entire county, including near the intersection of U.S. 301 and Sunset Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Michael tracked from South Carolina northeast and off the Mid Atlantic Coast from Thursday morning, October 11 into early Friday morning, October 12. The Tropical Cyclone produced heavy rain across much of central and south central Virginia, the Virginia Northern Neck, and the Middle Peninsula. Rainfall totals ranged between two and nine inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall total of 6.00 inches was reported at Hopewell (1 NE).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Michael tracked from South Carolina northeast and off the Mid Atlantic Coast from Thursday morning, October 11 into early Friday morning, October 12. Showers and scattered thunderstorms associated with the Tropical Cyclone produced heavy rain which caused flooding across portions of central and south central Virginia, the Virginia Northern Neck, and the Middle Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads remained impassable or closed across much of the county due to lingering flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Michael made landfall along the Florida panhandle as major hurricane (Category 4 on the Saffir-Simpson scale) on Wednesday afternoon, October 10th, 2018, then tracked northeastward with the northern portion of the storm circulation tracking across portions of Southside, Virginia Thursday afternoon, the 11th. A deep flow of tropical moisture into the Appalachians and central mid Atlantic region ahead of the storm began to generate excessive rainfall across portions of the region as early as Wednesday evening, mainly along the Interstate 77 corridor, causing some degree of flash flooding and otherwise saturating the ground even before the arrival of the rainfall directly associated with Michael.||As the storm circulation approached on Thursday, a cold front moving in from the west and interacted with the storm and enhanced rainfall especially east of Interstate 81. Widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported, along with local amounts over 10 inches, mainly from the mountains of North Carolina up through Southside Virginia. This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville.  Unfortunately, there were five fatalities as vehicles were swept away by flash flood waters in Pittsylvania County, Danville, and Charlotte County, Virginia.||The massive volume of water also then generated rapid rises on rivers including the New, Roanoke, and Dan. Major flooding was reported at several river gage locations along with a couple of record crests. There were a few landslides reported as well, blocking roads and in one case destroying a shed.||In addition, as the northern portion of the storm circulation moved across the Piedmont, bands of heavier rains/convection developed and mixed strong winds down to the surface.  Many trees were blown down in the water logged soil especially across Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties of Virginia where rainfall was heaviest.  Despite some initial reports of tornado sightings, damage surveys revealed no conclusive evidence of tornadic circulations and judged damage was caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS site at Brookneal 2.9 E (VA-CM-10) measured 4.90 inches for 24-hour period ending at 700 AM local time, October 12, 2018.

Widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported, along with local amounts over 10 inches, mainly from the mountains of North Carolina up through Southside Virginia. This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charlotte County was included in a Presidential Major Disaster declaration due mainly to the severe flooding associated with Tropical Storm Michael. There was widespread flash flooding across the county with numerous roads closed and damaged. Two people lost their lives after being swept off a bridge into floodwaters. The fatalities occurred after a car stranded on a Mt. Harmony Road bridge around 728 PM local Thursday. The Charlotte County Sheriff's office stated that the vehicle originally had three occupants who were said to be clinging to the bridge railings. A 17-year-old male was able to be rescued, while the two other adults, one male the other female were swept away in the flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roanoke River through Salem flooded due to the heavy rainfall from Michael and the predecessor rainfall event. Several roads were closed and businesses affected by the flood waters. The Riverwalk Apartments along the Roanoke River in Salem were evacuated and 42 units received flood damage. The crest at the Salem Pump Station IFLOWS gage (SPSV2) was 14.6 feet very early on the 12th, just over the Moderate flood stage of 14 feet and well above Minor flood stage of 7 feet. It was the 3rd highest crest at this gage in the existing record back to 1972 and the highest since the November 1985 flood when the river reached 20.5 feet at this location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roanoke County was included in a Presidential Major Disaster declaration due mainly to the severe flooding associated with Tropical Storm Michael. Extremely high sustained rain rates (2-4 inches per hour) became concentrated across parts of the Roanoke area between 1100 AM to 200 PM local time. At 243 PM a rare Flash Flood Emergency was issued for Roanoke City, City of Salem and Roanoke County after consultation with local Emergency management amidst reports of numerous water rescues in the area. Radar and rain gages showed 3 to 6 inches of rain during this period from the southern part of Roanoke City into the adjacent portions of Roanoke County. There were numerous reports of flash flooding across mainly the South County area where the heaviest rains fell, 5 to 6 inches in some locations. Mudlick Creek flooded across Cresthill Drive to a depth of at least one foot. Several small mudslides occurred and numerous roads were closed. There was also reports of high water rescues in several areas including a motorist stranded on Ferguson Valley Road along Back Creek. The USGS gage on Back Creek near Dundee (BCKV2) crested at 23.09 feet (17,000 cfs) in the evening of the 11th at the 2nd highest stage and discharge since records began in 1971 and only slightly below the record flood in November 1985 of 25. 1 feet.

This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville.  Unfortunately, there were five fatalities as vehicles were swept away by flash flood waters in Pittsylvania County, Danville, and Charlotte County, Virginia.||The massive volume of water also then generated rapid rises on rivers including the New, Roanoke, and Dan. Major flooding was reported at several river gage locations along with a couple of record crests. There were a few landslides reported as well, blocking roads and in one case destroying a shed.||In addition, as the northern portion of the storm circulation moved across the Piedmont, bands of heavier rains/convection developed and mixed strong winds down to the surface.  Many trees were blown down in the water logged soil especially across Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties of Virginia where rainfall was heaviest.  Despite some initial reports of tornado sightings, damage surveys revealed no conclusive evidence of tornadic circulations and judged damage was caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least twenty trees, as well as several power lines, were blown down across portions of Charlotte County as very heavy rainfall associated with thunderstorms which developed in one of the feeder bands of Tropical Storm Michael allowed strong winds aloft to mix down to the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Hurricane Michael made landfall near Mexico Beach, Florida with maximum sustained winds of 155 mph and a minimum central pressure of 919 mb on Wednesday October 10th, it weakened to a tropical storm as it tracked northeastward through Georgia and South Carolina. The tropical storm then tracked across North Carolina and southeast Virginia Thursday and Thursday night, October 11th, emerging back into the Atlantic Ocean early Friday morning, October 12th. Ahead of the tropical storm, the strong low level wind field combined with some instability ahead of the storm to generate bands of showers and thunderstorms, one of which became severe. As the system passed south of the region, heavy rain then caused instances of flooding, and strong winds brought down trees. Across Virginia, maximum sustained winds reached 35 mph, peak wind gusts reached 46 mph, and some rainfall totals were observed in excess of 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mallorys Ford Road was closed at Shelton Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Michael tracked from South Carolina northeast and off the Mid Atlantic Coast from Thursday morning, October 11 into early Friday morning, October 12. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads remained impassable or closed across much of the county due to lingering flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Michael made landfall along the Florida panhandle as major hurricane (Category 4 on the Saffir-Simpson scale) on Wednesday afternoon, October 10th, 2018, then tracked northeastward with the northern portion of the storm circulation tracking across portions of Southside, Virginia Thursday afternoon, the 11th. A deep flow of tropical moisture into the Appalachians and central mid Atlantic region ahead of the storm began to generate excessive rainfall across portions of the region as early as Wednesday evening, mainly along the Interstate 77 corridor, causing some degree of flash flooding and otherwise saturating the ground even before the arrival of the rainfall directly associated with Michael.||As the storm circulation approached on Thursday, a cold front moving in from the west and interacted with the storm and enhanced rainfall especially east of Interstate 81. Widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported, along with local amounts over 10 inches, mainly from the mountains of North Carolina up through Southside Virginia. This resulted in significant flash flooding with flash flood emergencies issued for the city of Roanoke, as well as Roanoke County, and southern Pittsylvania county including the city of Danville.  Unfortunately, there were five fatalities as vehicles were swept away by flash flood waters in Pittsylvania County, Danville, and Charlotte County, Virginia.||The massive volume of water also then generated rapid rises on rivers including the New, Roanoke, and Dan. Major flooding was reported at several river gage locations along with a couple of record crests. There were a few landslides reported as well, blocking roads and in one case destroying a shed.||In addition, as the northern portion of the storm circulation moved across the Piedmont, bands of heavier rains/convection developed and mixed strong winds down to the surface.  Many trees were blown down in the water logged soil especially across Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties of Virginia where rainfall was heaviest.  Despite some initial reports of tornado sightings, damage surveys revealed no conclusive evidence of tornadic circulations and judged damage was caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dan River at South Boston (SBNV2) crested at 31.36 feet (50900 cfs) on the afternoon of the 13th. Major flood stage is 29 feet. It was the 6th highest reading at this gage since records began in 1924 and the highest since September 8, 1996 (33.32 feet) during the remains of Hurricane Fran. In terms of flood frequency it fell near a 10-year event (0.10 annual chance flood). Significant flooding took place in the South Boston area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts estimated to be in the 25 to 35 mph range were common during the passage of Tropical Storm Michael. In addition, numerous power lines were blown down, resulting in several power outages across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 3 to 6 inches caused numerous creeks to escape their banks forcing the closure of several primary and secondary roads. The river gage on the Smith River near Woolwine (SMWV2) showed a rise in less than six hours to a crest of 12.15 feet, Minor flood stage is 9 feet and Moderate flood stage 11 feet. This was the 4th highest reading at this gage since data started there in 1995 and was roughly a 5-year annual recurrence interval (0.10 annual exceedance probability).
